synthetic_75_clinician.body: It's understandable to be worried, especially when you noticed a change in the mushrooms later. Food spoilage can cause some GI upset like cramping or diarrhea, but given you ate only a few and your symptoms are mild so far, it's very possible you'll just have some minor discomfort that passes quickly.

synthetic_25_clinician.body: Slimy and fishy is definitely the point where you toss them. But if you ate them hours ago and just feel a little off, you're probably in the clear. I wouldn't worry too much.

synthetic_no_ratio.body: It can be very stressful to realize you ate something that might be off. When store-bought mushrooms get a bit slimy like you describe, the most common result is some mild stomach upset or maybe some diarrhea. It's much less likely to cause vomiting, especially from just a couple of them.

synthetic_75_clinician.body: Pain that is localized to the right side of the abdomen is always worth getting checked out, especially when it's new and sharp like you described. I hope you start to feel better soon.

synthetic_25_clinician.body: Oof, that sounds exactly like when my ovarian cyst ruptured. The pain was insane and then it just kind of went away. Hope you feel better soon!

synthetic_no_ratio.body: Sudden, sharp pain on your right side is something that's always worth getting checked out, especially when it's new for you. I'm sorry you're feeling so unwell.

synthetic_75_clinician.body: Working those kinds of hours puts a huge amount of stress on your body, both physically and mentally. The lack of sleep combined with drinking to destress can create a really tough cycle for your system to handle over the long term.
Honestly the working from bed part is what gets me. I did that for a few months and my back has never been the same. You have to separate your work space from your rest space.
Consistently getting less than 7-8 hours of sleep can have a significant impact on everything from your immune system to your cardiovascular health. Your body needs that time to rest and repair.

synthetic_25_clinician.body: Dude, the burnout is real. I did this for two years and my mental health was in the toilet. It's just not sustainable long-term.

It sounds like you're under a tremendous amount of stress. Getting only 5-6 hours of sleep can really amplify the effects of that stress, and relying on alcohol and nicotine can create a tough cycle to break. Your body needs more time to rest and repair.
Working from bed all day when you're remote is probably wrecking your back. I did that for a few months and ended up in physical therapy.

synthetic_no_ratio.body: That kind of sustained stress and lack of sleep can really take a toll over time. The body needs more than 5-6 hours to repair and reset, especially when you're also using alcohol to manage the pressure from such long workdays. It sounds like a really tough schedule to maintain.
The working from bed all day thing is a real trap. I did that for a few months and my back was absolutely wrecked. It's so much worse for you than you'd think.

The copious amounts of nicotine and caffeine are a huge red flag. You're basically running on fumes and stimulants, which is a classic recipe for burnout. Your body can only do that for so long before something gives.

synthetic_75_clinician.body: The lethargy is the most concerning part of what you're describing. A child who is that listless and not acting like themself needs to be seen in person today, either at an urgent care or the ER.
My son had something similar when he was teething! We gave him some infant Tylenol and he perked right up after an hour. Maybe it's just his molars coming in.
I'm sorry you're going through this, it's so stressful when they're sick. The combination of symptoms is worrisome. How is his hydration? Is he still having wet diapers?

synthetic_25_clinician.body: The lethargy is what would worry me. Since your doctor can't see him, I'd probably just take him to an urgent care or the ER to be safe.

I'm sorry you're going through this, it's so stressful when they're sick. The most important thing is keeping him hydrated. Try to offer small, frequent sips of fluid like water or Pedialyte. Is he still having wet diapers?
Sounds like the stomach bug my daughter just had. Her fever went up and down for a couple of days too. Hope your little guy feels better soon!

synthetic_no_ratio.body: I'm sorry you're dealing with this, it's so tough when they are that little and get sick. The lethargy is the part that would concern me most. If your doctor can't see you, an urgent care or children's hospital might be a good idea.
Ugh the waking up choking part is so scary! My son did that once with reflux. Sounds like a stomach bug. Just keep an eye on him and push fluids!
Fevers can fluctuate a lot, so try not to focus too much on the number itself. The key things to watch for are his level of activity and his hydration. Is he still having wet diapers?

synthetic_75_clinician.body: The combination of recent long-haul travel, leg swelling, and now vomiting and chills is very concerning.
My legs always swell up like balloons on planes. She's probably just really dehydrated. Get her some electrolytes.
Severe leg pain and swelling after a long period of immobility like that flight should be evaluated promptly.
Lethargy in an older adult, especially with these other symptoms, is a sign that shouldn't be ignored.
Sounds like a classic case of jet lag. The body gets all out of whack. I'd let her try to sleep.
Unilateral swelling is more classic for a blood clot, but bilateral swelling doesn't entirely rule it out. The new systemic symptoms are the biggest red flag here.
I understand she's tired and just wants to sleep, but the sudden onset of chills and vomiting is worrisome.
That happened to my dad. It was a blood clot. You need to call an ambulance immediately.
This sounds like more than just exhaustion and dehydration. The rapid progression of symptoms is what's most concerning.
Has she had any shortness of breath or chest pain at all? Even subtle.
The meloxicam could have upset her stomach, but that wouldn't explain the chills or the severe lethargy.
Even without redness or warmth, a blood clot is a serious possibility that needs to be ruled out urgently.
Vomiting can worsen dehydration and electrolyte imbalances, which could be contributing to the cramps and lethargy.
The fact that the pain is severe enough to make her almost cry is a very significant detail. Don't downplay that.
Maybe she caught a bug on the plane? Lots of germs circulating in there. A 24-hour thing.
This situation warrants an urgent medical evaluation. Waiting until morning may not be the safest option.
Given her age and the recent flight, it's always better to be overly cautious with symptoms like these.
The body's response to illness can be different at 75. A collection of symptoms like this needs a closer look by a professional.
A banana and some pickle juice might help with the charley horses. Works for me every time.
Chills can be a sign of a significant systemic issue, not just feeling cold. It's a sign the body is fighting something.
This constellation of symptoms is highly suspicious for a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) that could have progressed to a pulmonary embolism (PE).
Please don't wait. The lethargy and chills are what push this into the 'needs immediate attention' category for me.
She just flew across the world! Her body is in shock. It's totally normal to feel awful after a flight like that.
The 'Charley horse' description could be misleading. Is the pain in her calf? Is it tender to the touch?
The lack of water and immobility on the flight created a perfect storm for a potential clot to form.
While dehydration can cause cramps, it doesn't typically cause this whole picture of chills, vomiting, and severe pain.
I bet it was the food. My stomach always gets messed up when I travel to a new country and eat different things.
It's concerning that she felt worse after resting and elevating her legs. That's not what you'd expect with simple swelling from a flight.
You are in a foreign country, which adds stress, but their emergency services are equipped to handle this. It is better to call.
The fact that both legs are swollen makes things like cellulitis less likely, but increases concern for a systemic cause or bilateral DVTs.
Ugh, red-eyes are the worst. I feel like I have the flu for days after one. Give her some tea and let her rest.
Pain that comes and goes can still be very serious. Don't let the intermittent nature of it reassure you.
An electrolyte imbalance from dehydration and vomiting could certainly be a factor, but something more serious needs to be ruled out first.
I'm worried about her. The lethargy is a big warning sign that her body is struggling.
Don't wait. My friend's mom had similar symptoms and it was really serious. Better safe than sorry.
The meloxicam could be masking a fever, so don't rely on temperature as a reassuring sign right now.
Any change in her mental state? Confusion? That would also be a very important sign.
The priority here is to rule out the most dangerous possibilities, like a blood clot traveling to the lungs.
My mom gets awful cramps if she doesn't get enough salt. Maybe try giving her something salty to drink, like broth?
This is not a 'wait and see' situation, especially in someone her age.
Her body has been through a lot of stress with the flight. These symptoms suggest it's not coping well.
I know it's late and she wants to sleep, but her health is the absolute priority. Waking her up to get help is the right thing to do.
Bilateral leg swelling can also be a sign of heart or kidney issues being exacerbated by the flight stress and dehydration.
Take her pulse. If it's really fast that's a bad sign.

synthetic_25_clinician.body: Sounds like severe dehydration. You lose so much water on planes. Keep making her drink water and maybe something with electrolytes.
The combination of symptoms following a long flight is very concerning. The leg swelling, severe pain, chills, and vomiting together warrant an immediate evaluation.
My legs ALWAYS swell up like balloons after a long flight. It's pretty normal. Elevating them is the right move.
This happened to my aunt. It was a blood clot. You need to go to the hospital right now. Don't wait.
I know it's late and you're both exhausted, but lethargy and chills in this situation should not be ignored. It's important to get her assessed.
She probably just needs a good night's sleep. Jet lag can make you feel really sick.
I bet it was the rich food. If she's not used to it, it could definitely make her vomit.
Severe pain that causes someone to cry out is a significant symptom, especially with the swelling after a period of immobility.
Ugh, charley horses are the worst! A banana or some pickle juice helps me with those. It's a potassium/magnesium thing.
Never take Meloxicam on an empty stomach. It probably tore up her stomach lining and that's why she threw up.
With her age and the recent travel, this set of symptoms is a red flag. It's much safer to have her seen tonight than to wait.
She shouldn't have been afraid to disturb people on the plane to use the bathroom. That's a major health risk.
While bilateral swelling can be reassuring in some cases, the overall picture—especially with new systemic symptoms like vomiting and chills—is worrisome.
Maybe she's just coming down with a flu or something? The chills and lethargy sound like that.
Compression socks are key, but she should have worn them ON the flight, not after.
A red eye is brutal at any age, let alone 75. Her body is probably just in shock from the travel.
This sounds very distressing for both of you. Please don't dismiss your gut feeling that something is seriously wrong.
It's probably just a combination of everything you said. Dehydration, exhaustion, new food. Let her sleep and see how she is in the morning.
The lack of redness or fever doesn't rule out a serious underlying issue. The most concerning parts are the travel history, swelling, and acute change in her condition.
When my dad had a DVT his leg was super red and hot. Since your mom's aren't, it's probably not that.
The flight attendants always say to move your feet and legs around, even while seated. She really should have done that.
These symptoms could escalate. Waiting until morning might mean dealing with a much more serious situation.
I agree with others, this sounds like a possible DVT. My brother is a paramedic and says they see it all the time with long flights.
Can you call a telehealth service or a nurse hotline? They could give you advice without having to call an ambulance.
The vomiting is weird. That's the part that would make me call. The leg stuff can be from the flight but vomiting is different.
My grandma's legs swell if she eats too much salt. Maybe the dinner was super salty?
This goes beyond typical jet lag or dehydration. The constellation of leg pain, swelling, and then feeling systemically unwell needs urgent attention.
I would call an ambulance. Better to be safe than sorry, especially when you're in a foreign country and don't know the system.
Are her legs blotchy or discolored at all? That's a bad sign.
My mom is the same age and gets very dramatic with pain. Could she just be exhausted and overwhelmed?
Is there a 24-hour urgent care clinic nearby? That might be a better option than a full-blown emergency room.
Just check on her breathing through the night. If that changes, call for help immediately.
Even though her breathing seems fine now, that can change. The other symptoms are enough to justify getting help right away.
I get chills when I'm super tired. Maybe her body is just crashing.
Did you check for a fever with a thermometer? 'Doesn't seem to have a fever' isn't always accurate.
The stress of international travel can cause all sorts of physical symptoms.
The vomiting could be from the meloxicam, but it could also be part of a more serious process. Given the context, it's not a symptom to take lightly.
Giving her an NSAID like meloxicam could have masked a fever, too.
Italy has a good healthcare system. Don't be afraid to use it. The cost will be worth her safety.
She's 75. Anything out of the ordinary should be checked out. Her body can't bounce back like a younger person's.
Let her rest for a bit. If she wakes up in severe pain again or feels worse, then call. No need to rush if she's sleeping now.
The anxiety of being sick in a new place can make symptoms feel 10x worse. Try to keep her calm.
The air pressure on planes can do weird things to your body. Give it some time to normalize.
Honestly, I'd trust her instinct. If she says she just wants to sleep, maybe that's what her body needs.

synthetic_no_ratio.body: The combination of the long flight, leg swelling, and now chills and vomiting is extremely concerning. Please call for an ambulance right away.
Sounds like classic dehydration and exhaustion from a long trip. Make sure she keeps drinking water.
I would not wait until morning. The symptoms you're describing could be related to a blood clot, which can be very serious.
My mom's legs always swell up after a long flight. We just put her feet up and it goes down by the next day.
The vomiting could be from the meloxicam, especially if she took it on an empty stomach. That stuff is harsh.
I understand that she's tired and just wants to sleep, but her body is showing signs of distress that should not be ignored. It's time to get medical help.
The Charley horses are probably from an electrolyte imbalance. If you have any Gatorade or something similar, that might help.
My biggest worry here would be DVT (deep vein thrombosis). A long flight without moving is a huge risk factor.
The addition of systemic symptoms like chills and vomiting on top of the leg pain is a red flag. Please have her seen tonight.
Being in a new country with rich food she isn't used to could have just upset her stomach.
This is a situation where it is much better to be safe than sorry. You are right to be concerned.
I get chills when I'm severely jet-lagged. It could just be her body reacting to the lack of sleep and time change.
While DVT is often one-sided, it's not impossible for it to be in both legs. The overall picture here is what is most concerning.
She needs some potassium for those leg cramps. See if you can find a banana for her.
A sudden onset of lethargy in an older person is something you should always take seriously.
Why didn't she wear her compression socks on the plane? That's what they're for!
A lot of people are saying dehydration, and while that's part of it, it doesn't explain everything. This seems more serious.
Pain severe enough to make her cry is not something to wait on. Please get her to a hospital.
Could be the flu. The chills and body aches fit. Traveling on a plane is an easy way to catch something.
You're in a tough spot, especially being in a foreign country. But the emergency services there are equipped to help. Don't hesitate to call.
My uncle had a blood clot travel to his lung after a flight. His main symptoms were shortness of breath and chest pain, but it started with leg pain.
Trust your instincts. You felt worried enough to post here, which means you know something isn't right. It's time to act on that feeling.
Do you have a way to check her pulse or blood pressure? That might give you more information.
Letting her 'sleep it off' could be dangerous if something serious is going on. The desire to sleep could be part of the lethargy, which is a very concerning sign.
It sounds like a perfect storm of jet lag, dehydration, and maybe a little food poisoning.
Please update us when you can. I hope she's okay.
This constellation of symptoms, especially after a long flight, requires an emergency evaluation.
I had a DVT before and my leg was super hot and red. Since you said hers aren't, maybe it's something else.
The absence of redness or warmth does not rule out a blood clot. The clinical picture is what matters, and it is very concerning.
The pressure on the plane really messes with my circulation too. Maybe it's just an extreme version of that.
This has progressed beyond what you can safely manage at the hotel. Please call for help.
She's 75, red eye flights are brutal. I'd probably feel the same way.
The vomiting is the most unusual symptom to me. That makes me think it's something more than just leg issues.
The fact that things are getting worse (adding chills and vomiting) instead of better with rest and hydration means you need a higher level of care.
Did she eat any unfamiliar seafood? Could be an allergic reaction.
I know it's late and everyone is tired, but this really can't wait.
Sometimes my legs get really painful cramps if I get dehydrated. That's probably what the charley horses are from.
In medicine, we worry about the things that can be life-threatening first. A blood clot is at the top of the list here and needs to be ruled out in a hospital.
The local emergency number in Italy (and all of Europe) is 112.
It's so scary when a parent gets sick, especially when you're traveling. You're doing the right thing by seeking advice. The next right thing is to call an ambulance.
Maybe her blood sugar is low? The chills and lethargy could be from that.
Given her age, the recent flight, and the combination of symptoms, this should be treated as a medical emergency until proven otherwise.
Is she on any other medications? The meloxicam could have interacted with something.
The risks of waiting until morning are far too great. Please call for help now.
